excerpt: Prayer is not without purity
Whosoever completes his ablution and then departs his house for the mosque to pray—that believer remains in a state of prayer until his ablution is broken
Water has been divided by Allah into three portions for the believers; because of these three divisions, each organ of ablution is repeated thrice
This is my ablution and the ablution of the Prophets whose leader I am

Purity is related to the purification of seven organs, of which four are obligatory and three are of the Sunna. Each act of purification, through the invitation, signifies both a spiritual and a physical range

Water passes through both sacred and defiled places. In sacred regions flow rivers, ponds, and wells that sustain life; in corrupted lands lie the bitter seas. When water traverses such defiled places, though its original essence is pure, the place transforms it through its impurity
source_location_hint: On the nature of pure water

excerpt: Purification is permissible only with pure water. Should any admixture enter the water—even perfume or syrup—then it becomes unsuitable for ablution or ritual bathing. Clean water removes filth, whereas filth cannot cleanse filth

The bucket from which the mustajīb draws water from the well represents the knowledge of the dāʿī, who reads the knowledge of the ḥujjat and teaches it to others. The vessel into which water is poured for ablution exemplifies the Dāʿī al-Muṭlaq, who teaches knowledge to the maʾdhūn and the makāsir
source_location_hint: On the symbolic meaning of ablution implements

excerpt: When the verse was revealed, the Messenger of Allah gathered Mawlānā ʿAlī, Mawlānā Fāṭima, Imām al-Ḥasan, and Imām al-Ḥusayn beneath his cloak and declared these are my People of the House. The noble Salma requested inclusion but was told none else could be added save these five persons
source_author: Transmitted through Imamī and Ismaili sources
source_location_hint: On the occasion of Verse of Purification

excerpt: None of the Imāms require physical cleansing, for they are free from any impurity. Yet they purified themselves in order to remove defects that had accumulated in their veils through knowledge. Their purity was in truth the purification of their veils
source_location_hint: On the nature of Imāmic purity

excerpt: The Heavens refer to the Prophet of this age, Muḥammad al-Muṣṭafā; the earth refers to his successor, the Commander of the Believers ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib. Water signifies the knowledge that the recipient acquires, and purity means freedom from all manner of doubt
source_work: Taʾwīl al-Daʿāʾim or related exegetical works

excerpt: The Prophet, upon whom be peace, pointed to his successor ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, declaring the earth is your mother and she does good to you. When ʿAlī received this knowledge from the Prophet, he remained pure and untainted, becoming a means of growth and development for the souls of believers
source_location_hint: On the symbolism of earth and ʿAlī

excerpt: Concerning the corrupt earth, al-Muʾayyad says: O people drowsy in negligence, who dwell in the lap of a mother about to devour her own children—why do you sit in ease in a house destined for destruction

There are two types of water that clouds shed from the sky. Some clouds thunder but do not rain; others are heavy with water and pour upon the earth. The cloud is a symbol of the dāʿī. Those clouds that rain are like the Summoners of the Pen who write books and spread sciences. Those clouds that merely thunder resemble the Summoners of the Sword who fight with the sword
source_location_hint: On clouds as symbols of summoners

excerpt: In the early days of Islam the Messenger of Allah used to wash his head and feet. But after the event of the Night Journey, when the verse commanding wiping was revealed, he changed this practice at Allah's command and began to wipe his head and feet. His successor understood the gesture and perceived the Prophet's purpose; he too anointed his head and feet
source_location_hint: On the change from washing to wiping

excerpt: In the early stages of his education, the Prophet had not yet truly comprehended the hierarchy of the Universal Intellect and the Universal Soul. They seemed to him as if they were bounded and corporeal. When his station as the Speaker was perfected and he ascended in spirit to the heavens, the Holy Prophet perceived these spiritual limits as they truly are. Thereafter, in every act of ablution, he only wiped the head and feet, demonstrating to the Umma that these two limits are not corporeally tangible but rational and intelligible

Imām Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq said: Religious concealment is my religion and the religion of my fathers. Yet there are three matters in which taqiyya is not permissible: in the use of intoxicating substances, in wiping over socks, and in abandoning the recitation of Bismillāh al-Raḥmān al-Raḥīm in prayer. It is forbidden for one who believes that wiping over socks is permissible, for he will pray without complete ablution, as if he were not praying at all

At the beginning of his Prophethood, the Prophet used to wipe his feet and head through his socks and turban, for the truth concerning the Universal Intellect and the Universal Soul had not yet been unveiled to him. When Gabriel the Faithful revealed this truth and he attained knowledge of their hierarchy, he wiped his head and bare feet. His successor, perceiving this reality and accepting his command, ceased wiping over the turban and socks
source_location_hint: On the revelation of spiritual realities

excerpt: The term al-ghāʾiṭ refers to a low-lying place on earth where one can hide while defecating, unseen by others. The inner meaning suggests that the purification of the Respondent through inner knowledge should occur independently, without others' awareness that the Respondent is acquiring this knowledge
source_location_hint: On the esoteric meaning of impurity

excerpt: Touching women refers esoterically to the transmission of knowledge between a beneficent teacher and a learner, requiring purification of both. Intercourse can be lawful or unlawful. If the beneficiary acquires knowledge from one authorized to teach, as if through lawful marriage, the teaching is lawful; otherwise it is unlawful
source_location_hint: On the symbolism of intercourse and knowledge transmission

excerpt: If the respondent does not find access to one of the highest limits of the daʿwa, then it is permissible for him to gain knowledge from a believer near to him whose religion and faith are sound. Even one of the higher limits of the daʿwa may encounter an elder who embodies the likeness of water—that is, pure knowledge
